steps.title: Find the key assessment
steps.content:
Alfonzo argues that “our understanding of individuals' decision-making about whether to walk is insufficiently robust”—meaning current explanations are incomplete.

This judgment signals why the passage lists demographic characteristics and sidewalk width: to show what studies have emphasized as important, but revealing their insufficiency.

steps.title: Match the assessment to the choices
steps.content:
Choice B perfectly matches this function—it identifies these references as factors studies have recognized as important influences, yet emphasizes they're “merely some of the many factors” that shape walking decisions.

The other three either claim the passage asserts these factors are broadly relevant to people (A), rarely considered by individuals (C), or unique to Rome (D).

That matches choice B — B is correct.
